skill.name: iot-uart-console-picocom
skill.description: Guides authorized IoT hardware testers through UART serial console access with picocom and a Python serial helper.
skill.summary: Connect to and document IoT UART serial consoles during authorized security testing.
skill.author: BrownFineSecurity

security_audit.summary: Static findings for command execution, filesystem access, network use, and credential access are largely true positives in the skill documentation. The content is framed as authorized IoT pentesting, so I did not find confirmed malicious intent, but it includes high-risk persistence, credential access, privilege escalation, and exfiltration guidance that is not safe for marketplace publication without substantial guardrails.

high_findings.title: Persistence and Backdoor Instructions
high_findings.description: The skill instructs users to add SSH authorized keys, create a UID 0 backdoor user, and add startup entries for continued access. These steps can create unauthorized persistent control of an embedded device.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line ranges explicitly describe persistence methods, including SSH keys, a backdoor user, and a reverse shell startup script. The surrounding context says pentesting, but the operational steps are directly dangerous.

high_findings.title: Credential and Secret Harvesting Guidance
high_findings.description: The skill directs users to read password databases, sudoers data, keys, password files, and API keys. This can expose credentials and secrets from target systems.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited sections explicitly list /etc/shadow, /etc/sudoers, credential files, password searches, and api_key searches. These are real sensitive-data access patterns, not scanner-only matches.

high_findings.title: Privilege Escalation and Shell Escape Playbooks
high_findings.description: The documentation provides concrete shell escape, SUID abuse, sudo abuse, and bootloader modification steps to gain root shells. This is high-risk dual-use exploitation guidance.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The evidence shows direct root-shell techniques through boot arguments, SUID binaries, pagers, editors, scripting languages, and sudo. These steps are intentional exploitation workflows.

high_findings.title: Firmware Extraction and Network Transfer
high_findings.description: The skill includes commands to dump flash partitions and transfer root filesystem images over netcat to an external listener. This can enable data exfiltration from devices.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited examples show dd reads from MTD partitions and netcat transfer to an attacker or external IP. Firmware extraction can be legitimate in a lab, but this is clear exfiltration-capable guidance.

medium_findings.title: Arbitrary Serial Command Automation
medium_findings.description: The helper accepts single commands, interactive input, and script files, then sends them to the attached device. This is expected for the skill, but it can automate harmful target-side actions.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The helper does not execute these commands on the host shell, which reduces host risk. It still enables arbitrary command execution on a connected device through the serial interface.

medium_findings.title: Sensitive Session Logging
medium_findings.description: The helper and documentation log all serial I/O, including commands and device responses. Logs may capture passwords, keys, tokens, or proprietary device data.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Logging is an intentional feature and useful for audits, but the cited lines confirm raw sent and received serial data is stored. UART sessions commonly include sensitive information.

low_findings.title: Benign Serial Device and Temporary File References
low_findings.description: Many low and medium static hits are expected references to /dev/ttyUSB devices, /tmp log files, and local serial tooling. These are normal for a UART console skill but still require user permission and log hygiene.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The locations are consistent with legitimate serial-console setup and observation workflows. They are not host malware by themselves, but they contribute operational risk.

dangerous_patterns.title: UID 0 Account Creation
dangerous_patterns.description: The examples append backdoor account entries with UID 0 to /etc/passwd, including a no-password variant.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command text and surrounding headings explicitly describe adding a backdoor user. This is a direct persistence pattern.

dangerous_patterns.title: Reverse Shell on Boot
dangerous_patterns.description: The examples create an init script that repeatedly starts an interactive shell to an attacker-controlled TCP endpoint.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The evidence shows a startup persistence mechanism and outbound shell command. It is explicitly labeled as a backdoor-related method.

dangerous_patterns.title: Password and Key File Discovery
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill searches password, credential, PEM, and key files across the device filesystem.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The patterns are directly present as credential and key discovery commands. Legitimate audit use is possible, but the data-access risk is clear.

dangerous_patterns.title: Root Shell via Boot Arguments
dangerous_patterns.description: The documentation modifies U-Boot boot arguments to launch a root shell without authentication.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The lines provide a concrete bootloader path to bypass normal authentication. This is a high-impact exploitation technique.

dangerous_patterns.title: Netcat Firmware Transfer
dangerous_patterns.description: The examples transfer dumped firmware or root filesystem data over netcat to an external listener.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands show raw partition dumps followed by network transfer. This is exfiltration-capable even when used in authorized firmware analysis.

content.value_statement: IoT hardware testing often requires reliable access to UART consoles. This skill provides serial connection workflows, logging guidance, and security test checklists for authorized assessments.
actual_capabilities: Explains how to connect to UART devices with picocom and common baud rates.
Provides a Python helper for sending serial commands and reading console output.
Documents session logging methods for observing and preserving serial I/O.
Lists common IoT console prompts, BusyBox commands, and bootloader workflows.
Includes checklists for authorized enumeration, firmware review, and reporting.
limitations: Requires physical UART access and compatible serial hardware.
Does not verify that the user has authorization for a target device.
Contains dual-use exploitation and persistence examples that need human oversight.
Can damage or alter devices if bootloader or filesystem commands are misused.

use_cases.title: Authorized Hardware Assessment
use_cases.description: Connect to a client-owned device, capture console output, and document exposed services or weak settings.

use_cases.title: Firmware Lab Validation
use_cases.description: Use UART access to inspect boot logs, shell behavior, and bootloader controls in a controlled lab.

use_cases.title: Device Debugging Workflow
use_cases.description: Set up repeatable serial logging and command execution while troubleshooting embedded Linux devices.

prompt_templates.title: Connect to a UART Console
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me connect to my authorized IoT device over UART using picocom. Ask for the serial device path and baud rate first.

prompt_templates.title: Log a Serial Session
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a safe workflow to log all UART console input and output for an authorized device assessment.

prompt_templates.title: Enumerate Device State
prompt_templates.prompt: Guide me through non-destructive UART enumeration for a device I own. Focus on identity, firmware version, services, and permissions.

prompt_templates.title: Review Risky Test Steps
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this UART test plan for destructive, persistence, credential access, or exfiltration steps before I run it in my lab.

output_examples.input: I need to observe a UART session while another terminal runs commands.
output_examples.output: The skill would suggest enabling session logging, tailing the log from a second terminal, and recording the device path and baud rate.

output_examples.input: My authorized test device shows a BusyBox shell.
output_examples.output: The skill would provide a focused enumeration sequence for firmware version, mounted filesystems, processes, and network services.

output_examples.input: I want to interrupt U-Boot on my lab board.
output_examples.output: The skill would explain how to capture boot output, identify the bootloader prompt, and document boot settings before changes.

best_practices: Use this skill only on devices you own or have written permission to test.
Prefer read-only enumeration before modifying bootloader settings, files, or accounts.
Store UART logs securely because they can contain credentials, keys, and device secrets.
anti_patterns: Do not use persistence, reverse shell, or backdoor examples outside an approved lab scope.
Do not extract firmware, credentials, or keys from third-party devices without authorization.
Do not run bootloader or filesystem write commands without a recovery plan.

faq.question: Does this skill require hardware access?
faq.answer: Yes. It assumes a UART adapter, a connected target device, and permission to access the device.

faq.question: Can it run commands on my host system?
faq.answer: The helper runs locally, but its main purpose is sending commands through a serial console to the attached device.

faq.question: Why is this skill high risk?
faq.answer: It includes instructions for credential access, privilege escalation, persistence, and firmware transfer.

faq.question: Is it suitable for production devices?
faq.answer: Use it cautiously. Some bootloader and filesystem commands can alter or damage devices.

faq.question: Does it include logging?
faq.answer: Yes. The helper and documentation describe serial session logging for later review.

faq.question: What tools does it reference?
faq.answer: It references picocom, pyserial, BusyBox utilities, socat, screen, and common Linux diagnostic commands.
